quote: Were not our hearts burning within us as he spoke to us?
firstReading: In Acts 3:1-10 Peter and John encounter a man lame from birth at the Beautiful Gate. The man begs for alms, but Peter, invoking the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, commands him to rise and walk. In the name of Jesus the man is healed instantly: he stands, walks, and enters the temple with them, praising God. The crowd is filled with wonder and amazement at this visible sign of God’s power.
psalmSummary: Psalm 105 calls the assembly to give thanks, to seek the Lord and remember his wondrous deeds. It urges praise for God’s enduring covenant with Abraham and the chosen people, celebrating God’s guidance and providential care. The psalm emphasizes the faithfulness and mercy of God who keeps his promises, inviting the faithful to recount his works and to center their hope on the steadfast love that shapes salvation history.
gospelSummary: Luke 24:13-35 narrates the road to Emmaus: two disciples leave Jerusalem, discussing recent events, when the risen Jesus joins them unrecognized. He interprets Scripture concerning the Messiah, opening their minds to understand how suffering and resurrection fulfill God’s plan. Their hearts burn as he speaks, but recognition comes only at the breaking of the bread, when he vanishes from their sight. They return at once to Jerusalem to report the encounter, transformed and aflame with testimony.
saintReflection: St. Julie Billiart (Optional Memorial): St. Julie Billiart (1751-1816) was a French woman who, despite being paralyzed for 22 years and surviving the terror of the French Revolution, founded the Sisters of Notre Dame de Namur to educate young girls. She was miraculously cured of her paralysis and spent the rest of her life tirelessly expanding her order, always repeating her motto: 'How good is the good God.'
dailyPrayer: Lord Jesus, you who healed in your name and opened the Scriptures to the disciples on the road to Emmaus, come to our hearts. Grant us the grace to recognize you in the Scriptures, in the breaking of the bread, and in our neighbor’s need. Like Peter and John, make us instruments of your mercy, that those who long for healing may know your touch. Inspire in us the faithful service of St. Julie Billiart, that we might teach, comfort, and serve with patience and trust. May our hearts burn with your word and our hands work for your kingdom, so every act of charity bears witness to your resurrection. Amen.
theologicalSynthesis: These readings weave a single pastoral and theological thread: the risen Lord is present, recognized in word and sacrament, and his power transforms lives. In Acts the healing at the temple gate attests to Christ’s continuing work through apostolic witness, validating the community’s mission. Psalm 105 situates that work within covenantal faithfulness: God’s promises endure and call for thanksgiving. Luke’s Emmaus scene shows how Scripture and Eucharist disclose the paschal mystery, opening hearts to mission. St. Julie’s life models this dynamic: her trust in God’s goodness leads to educational and pastoral outreach. Together, they invite a Church that reads, breaks bread, heals, and bears witness to God’s steadfast love in concrete service.
exegesis:
Context: Acts 3 occurs in the earliest phase of the Jerusalem community, shortly after Pentecost and the initial proclamation of Jesus’ resurrection. The narrative sets a public scene at the temple gate, a liminal space where religious life and daily need intersect. Peter and John are figures of apostolic authority, not as magicians but as witnesses who wield the name of Jesus as the source of transformative power. The episode answers the question of how the risen Christ continues to act: not by returning to earthly ministry but by empowering the community to enact signs that confirm the gospel.

Psalm: Psalm 105 functions as a liturgical memory, urging the community to remember God’s mighty deeds and covenant fidelity. The selected verses call the faithful to praise, to seek the face of the Lord, and to recall the election of Abraham. In relation to the Acts story, the psalm supplies the theological atmosphere: God who has acted in history continues to be faithful; public acts of deliverance—now in the form of miraculous healing—are interpreted as part of the same saving plan that has guided Israel. The psalm frames wonder as the appropriate response to God’s enduring mercy.

Gospel: Luke’s Emmaus narrative is a compact catechesis on recognition. Jesus reveals himself through two channels: the interpretation of Scripture and the Eucharistic breaking of bread. The disciples’ inability to recognize him earlier underscores spiritual blindness, remedied by the pedagogical unfolding of God’s plan. The phrase about their hearts burning captures the interior movement from confusion to conviction. The risen Lord’s vanishing at recognition shifts focus: the encounter demands proclamation; the disciples leave to join the community with renewed testimony, showing how encounter leads to mission.

Acts and Luke together: Reading Acts 3 alongside Luke 24 highlights complementary modes of encounter. Acts shows the outward sign—healing—that invites public astonishment and debate; Luke emphasizes the inward conversion prompted by Scripture and sacrament. Both insist that the resurrection is not merely private consolation but the cause of communal witness. Peter’s use of Jesus’ name echoes the Emmaus recognition: the power of Christ’s identity is operative when proclaimed and embodied. The early Church’s authority thus derives from faithfulness to the paschal revelation and its sacramental mediation.

Saints: St. Julie Billiart offers a concrete prolongation of these themes. Her life lived the rhythm of encounter, recognition, and service. Paralyzed and persecuted, she experienced God’s fidelity and then responded by founding a religious family dedicated to education and the spiritual transformation of young people. Her ministry incarnated the Acts pattern—public, observable change fueled by trust in God—and the Luke pattern—formation through word and sacrament that deepens interior devotion and leads to social outreach. Her motto, 'How good is the good God,' signals a providential theology that shapes pastoral courage.

Today: For contemporary believers, these texts prompt practical questions: Where do we expect to meet the risen Lord—at liturgy, in Scripture study, in acts of mercy? How does communal testimony validate our personal encounters? The healing at the Beautiful Gate challenges communities to care for bodily and spiritual need; Emmaus insists on the primacy of Scripture and Eucharist as the loci of recognition and commissioning. In parishes and schools, the legacy of saints like Julie urges formation that marries doctrine with compassion. The Church’s credibility rests on visible signs of charity grounded in sacramental life; faithful witness continues the apostolic mission, inviting others to discover that the God of the covenant remains merciful and present.

Conclusion: Together, these readings form a pastoral cycle: remembrance, encounter, recognition, and mission. The risen Christ discloses himself in ways that lead to active witness—words confirmed by deeds. The faithful are called to cultivate receptive hearts, to proclaim boldly in word and service, and to allow sacramental worship to convert life into testimony of God’s enduring goodness.
